methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $15M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Defense & Security. Range: $150M - $500M.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Defense & Dual-use
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Defense & Dual-use

private.name: Stark Defence
private.rationale: Same sector (Defense & Security), same stage (Series A), post-money $500M

private.name: Vatn Systems
private.rationale: Same sector (Defense & Security), same stage (Series A), post-money $300M

private.name: Ares Industries
private.rationale: Same sector (Defense & Security), same stage (Series A), post-money $150M

data.narrative:
SiFly is a Series A-stage Defense & Security company that has raised $15M+ to date. The valuation analysis draws on a comparable universe of 10 private and 2 public defense-tech companies, including same-stage peers Stark Defence ($500M), Vatn Systems ($300M), and Ares Industries ($150M).

Two triangulation methods were applied. Stage-progression analysis, using total capital raised as a baseline proxy of $15M and applying a typical 2.0x step-up multiplier for Series A advancement, implies a valuation of $30M. Peer-median analysis across the three same-sector, same-stage comparables yields a median valuation of $300M, with a observed range of $150M to $500M. The dispersion reflects variance in technology maturity, customer traction, and market positioning within the defense subsector.

Synthesizing both methods produces a triangulated range of $26M to $345M, with a median of $165M. The wide band reflects material uncertainty around SiFly's relative competitive position and revenue trajectory within a peer set that spans nearly 3.5x from floor to ceiling. Stage-progression anchors conservatively near the capital-raised baseline, while peer-median suggests substantially higher value if SiFly matches the technical or commercial standing of mid-to-upper-quartile comparables. Key sensitivities include customer concentration, technology differentiation versus incumbents, and near-term revenue visibility.
